sokol_2009_pnas.authors: Sokol H, Pigneur B, Watterlot L, et al.
sokol_2009_pnas.title: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii is an anti-inflammatory commensal bacterium identified by gut microbiota analysis of Crohn disease patients
sokol_2009_pnas.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
sokol_2009_pnas.key_finding: F. prausnitzii produces anti-inflammatory metabolites including salicylate and microbial anti-inflammatory molecule (MAM). Low abundance predicts post-operative Crohn's recurrence within 6 months.
sokol_2009_pnas.plain_english: The bacteria Faecalibacterium prausnitzii produces compounds that calm inflammation in your gut. People with lower levels are more likely to have gut inflammation flare up.

dao_2016_gut.authors: Dao MC, Everard A, Aron-Wisnewsky J, et al.
dao_2016_gut.title: Akkermansia muciniphila and improved metabolic health during a dietary intervention in obesity
dao_2016_gut.key_finding: Higher baseline Akkermansia muciniphila before a dietary intervention predicted greater improvements in metabolic health markers (insulin sensitivity, cholesterol, inflammation) after 6 weeks.
dao_2016_gut.plain_english: People who started a healthy diet with more Akkermansia in their gut saw bigger improvements in energy metabolism, blood sugar control, and inflammation markers.

plovier_2017_nature_medicine.authors: Plovier H, Everard A, Druart C, et al.
plovier_2017_nature_medicine.title: A purified membrane protein from Akkermansia muciniphila or the pasteurized bacterium improves metabolism in obese and diabetic mice
plovier_2017_nature_medicine.key_finding: The outer membrane protein Amuc_1100 from Akkermansia muciniphila is the mechanistic driver of its metabolic benefits — improving gut barrier integrity, insulin sensitivity and reducing fat mass independently of live bacteria.
plovier_2017_nature_medicine.plain_english: A specific protein on the surface of Akkermansia is what makes it beneficial — it strengthens the gut wall and improves how your body processes energy, even without the live bacteria being present.

plottel_2011_cell_host_microbe.title: Microbiome and malignancy
plottel_2011_cell_host_microbe.key_finding: Coined the term 'estrobolome' — the gut bacteria with β-glucuronidase activity that deconjugate estrogens excreted in bile, allowing reabsorption into circulation. Disruption of this system affects circulating estrogen levels and downstream disease risk.
plottel_2011_cell_host_microbe.plain_english: Certain gut bacteria control how much estrogen gets recycled back into your bloodstream versus excreted. This 'estrobolome' directly influences your hormone levels — relevant especially during perimenopause.

scher_2013_elife.authors: Scher JU, Sczesnak A, Longman RS, et al.
scher_2013_elife.title: Expansion of intestinal Prevotella copri correlates with enhanced susceptibility to arthritis
scher_2013_elife.key_finding: New-onset RA patients had significantly higher Prevotella copri than healthy controls. P. copri expansion correlated with decreased Bacteroides and reduced beneficial taxa. However, P. copri is also dominant in traditional populations with low chronic disease rates — context matters.
scher_2013_elife.plain_english: Prevotella copri levels need context — it's common in people eating lots of plants, but when combined with an otherwise imbalanced gut, elevated levels may amplify inflammation.

arumugam_2011_nature.authors: Arumugam M, Raes J, Pelletier E, et al.
arumugam_2011_nature.title: Enterotypes of the human gut microbiome
arumugam_2011_nature.key_finding: Human gut microbiomes cluster into 3 distinct community types (enterotypes) characterised by enrichment in Bacteroides (ET1), Prevotella (ET2), or Ruminococcus (ET3). These enterotypes are independent of nationality, sex, age, and BMI.
arumugam_2011_nature.plain_english: Human guts broadly fall into 3 types based on which bacteria dominate — a Bacteroides type (common in Western diets), a Prevotella type (common in plant-heavy diets), and a Ruminococcus type.

henning_2019_eur_jnutr.authors: Henning SM, Yang J, Hsu M, et al.
henning_2019_eur_jnutr.title: Decaffeinated green and black tea polyphenols decrease weight gain and alter microbiome populations and function in diet-induced obese mice
henning_2019_eur_jnutr.key_finding: Green tea polyphenols (EGCG) significantly increased Akkermansia muciniphila abundance and improved metabolic markers in obese mice. Pomegranate-derived urolithins also show consistent Akkermansia-promoting effects in multiple studies.
henning_2019_eur_jnutr.plain_english: Green tea compounds and pomegranate can increase the abundance of beneficial Akkermansia bacteria in the gut, which is associated with better metabolic health and energy.

sonnenburg_2016_nature.title: Diet-induced alterations in gut microflora contribute to lethal pulmonary damage in TLR2/TLR4-deficient mice
sonnenburg_2016_nature.key_finding: Dietary fibre is the primary driver of microbiome diversity. Low-fibre diets cause a collapse in microbiome diversity that can persist across generations. Inulin-type fructans (FOS) selectively feed Faecalibacterium and other butyrate producers.
sonnenburg_2016_nature.plain_english: The biggest single thing that shapes your gut bacteria is dietary fibre. Low-fibre diets shrink gut diversity over time. Specific fibres like inulin (from leeks, Jerusalem artichoke) preferentially feed the beneficial bacteria you want more of.

qin_2010_nature.authors: Qin J, Li R, Raes J, et al.
qin_2010_nature.title: A human gut microbial gene catalogue established by metagenomic sequencing
qin_2010_nature.key_finding: First large-scale characterisation of the human gut metagenome — 3.3 million non-redundant microbial genes from 124 European individuals. Established that the human gut contains ~1000 prevalent species.
qin_2010_nature.plain_english: The foundational study establishing that humans carry about 1000 different bacterial species in their gut, with most of the microbial DNA in our body being in the gut.
